steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is an indirect (embedded) question punctuation and word-order question. The sentence says the team set out “to investigate how ______” — the blank finishes an indirect question embedded inside a declarative sentence. An indirect question must use statement word order (subject before verb) and end with a period, not a question mark.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — could their use be better regulated?: Inverted word order (auxiliary before subject) turns this into a direct question, which is grammatically wrong inside an indirect-question structure. The question mark compounds the error. ✗
  • Choice B — their use could be better regulated.: Statement word order (subject their use before verb could be regulated) is correct for an indirect question, and the period correctly ends the declarative sentence. ✓
  • Choice C — their use could be better regulated?: The word order is correct, but a question mark cannot end a declarative sentence; only a period is appropriate here. ✗
  • Choice D — could their use be better regulated.: Inverted word order treats the clause as a direct question, violating the indirect-question rule, even though the period is used. ✗
That is the correct answer.
